summary: Fowler reflects on a decade-old Thoughtworks project that has successfully avoided becoming a legacy morass through good design and continuous improvement. The project, which started with J2EE in 1998, has survived EJB removal, offshore transitions, and significant team turnover while remaining productive. The team evolved their acceptance testing approach from story-to-test tracing to a coherent specification-by-example suite. Most notably, new developer onboarding time dropped from one year to three months, demonstrating improved code quality over time.
key_points: Long-term business value delivery enabled by well-designed code is the hallmark of successful software development
Acceptance test suites should describe application behavior holistically rather than trace one-to-one with user stories, reducing duplication and complexity
Developer onboarding time serves as a practical metric for code quality and design health
A ten-year-old codebase can improve rather than decay if the team continuously invests in its design
Good design's purpose is enabling continued rapid development over time, not just initial elegance
money_quotes: The fact that they are still adding useful business value after ten years is a big dollop of kudos.
They are able to rapidly add new features when needed so haven't fallen into the typical morass of a legacy app.

A few years ago, if they wanted to take on someone new they wanted that person committed for at least a year, so they could get contributions that would be worthwhile after coming up to speed on the code base. Now that time is down to three months.
For me the key purpose of good design is that it allows you to continue working rapidly with the code.
Assessing how long it takes a developer to be productive with a code base is a good way to sense this design quality.
key_insight: The true measure of good software design is not initial elegance but whether a codebase remains productive and improvable over years of development and team turnover.
